questions.stem: What resources are required for pre-training a foundation model?

options.text: Millions of GPU compute hours, terabytes to petabytes of data, trillions of tokens, trial and error, and significant time
A single GPU, a few gigabytes of labeled data, and a few hours
Only CPU compute and a small curated dataset of thousands of examples
No compute resources — pre-training is done entirely through prompt engineering

explanation.correct: Pre-training is a complex process requiring millions of GPU compute hours, terabytes and petabytes of data, trillions of tokens, trial and error, and time. This is why pre-training from scratch is the most expensive approach.

distractors.B: A single GPU with gigabytes of data is suitable for fine-tuning small models, not pre-training a foundation model from scratch.
distractors.C: Pre-training requires massive GPU compute, not just CPUs. It also uses unstructured data at petabyte scale, not small curated sets.
distractors.D: Pre-training is a computational training process. Prompt engineering works with already-trained models at inference time.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of fine-tuning with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)?

options.text: To use reinforcement learning to better align the model with human preferences, generating human-like responses to human-like prompts
To replace all model weights with manually coded rules from human experts
To train the model exclusively on synthetic data generated by another AI
To reduce the model's parameter count for faster inference

explanation.correct: RLHF uses reinforcement learning to fine-tune the LLM with human feedback data, better aligning the model with human preferences. It improves the model's ability to understand human-like prompts and generate human-like responses.

distractors.B: RLHF updates model weights through reinforcement learning signals, not by replacing them with hand-coded rules.
distractors.C: RLHF specifically uses human feedback (preferences, rankings) as its training signal, not purely synthetic data.
distractors.D: RLHF does not reduce parameters. It refines model behavior through reward signals without changing architecture.

questions.stem: When might catastrophic forgetting be acceptable in a fine-tuning project?

options.text: When you need reliable performance on only a single task and are not concerned about the model generalizing to other tasks
When you want the model to perform well on all possible tasks
When the model will be used by many teams for diverse purposes
When regulatory requirements mandate multi-task capability

explanation.correct: If you need reliable performance on a single task, you might not be concerned that the model can't generalize to other tasks. In this case, catastrophic forgetting is an acceptable tradeoff for optimized single-task performance.

distractors.B: If you need performance across all tasks, catastrophic forgetting is a serious problem. Multitask fine-tuning would be needed.
distractors.C: Multiple teams with diverse needs require general capability. Catastrophic forgetting would harm their varied use cases.
distractors.D: Regulatory multi-task requirements would make catastrophic forgetting unacceptable.

questions.stem: What is the output of multitask fine-tuning?

options.text: An instruction-tuned model that has learned how to complete many different tasks simultaneously
A model that can only perform the last task it was trained on
A model with all its original weights removed
A model that requires no prompts to generate output

explanation.correct: Multitask fine-tuning produces an instruction-tuned model that has learned how to complete many different tasks simultaneously — reviews, summarization, translation, code, and more.

distractors.B: That describes catastrophic forgetting from sequential single-task fine-tuning. Multitask training retains multiple capabilities.
distractors.C: Weights are updated during multitask fine-tuning, not removed. The model retains and extends its parameters.
distractors.D: Instruction-tuned models still need prompts. They've learned to follow instructions across multiple tasks.

questions.stem: Why does continuous pre-training make model validation more difficult?

options.text: The output of generative AI models is non-deterministic, making it harder to validate correctness with fixed expected outputs
Continuous pre-training deletes all previous model weights
Validation datasets cannot be stored in Amazon S3
Continuous pre-training only works on image models, not text

explanation.correct: The output of generative AI models is non-deterministic, which makes validation more difficult. You need to choose appropriate metrics, benchmarks, and datasets to evaluate capabilities and ensure no harmful outputs.

distractors.B: Continuous pre-training extends training, it doesn't delete weights. The challenge is evaluating non-deterministic outputs.
distractors.C: S3 is the standard storage for training and validation datasets. This is not a limitation.
distractors.D: Continuous pre-training works on text models. Amazon Bedrock supports it for Titan Text models specifically.

questions.stem: What are the key differences between pre-training and fine-tuning? (Select TWO)

options.text: Pre-training uses self-supervised learning on unstructured data; fine-tuning uses supervised learning on labeled examples
Pre-training requires massive compute (millions of GPU hours); fine-tuning extends existing training with less compute
Pre-training produces a task-specific model; fine-tuning produces a general-purpose model
Pre-training requires labeled data; fine-tuning uses unlabeled data
Both require identical amounts of data and compute

explanation.correct: Pre-training uses self-supervised learning on huge unstructured data requiring millions of GPU hours. Fine-tuning is supervised learning on labeled examples that extends the pre-trained model with far less compute.

distractors.C: This is reversed. Pre-training produces a general-purpose model; fine-tuning adapts it for specific tasks.
distractors.D: This is reversed. Pre-training uses unstructured (unlabeled) data; fine-tuning uses labeled examples.
distractors.E: Pre-training requires dramatically more data and compute than fine-tuning.

questions.stem: How does multitask fine-tuning help mitigate catastrophic forgetting? (Select TWO)

options.text: The training dataset contains examples of inputs and outputs for multiple tasks simultaneously
Losses calculated from diverse task examples update weights in a way that preserves multi-task capability
It freezes all model weights so no forgetting can occur
It deletes task-specific neurons after each training epoch
It replaces the model with a separate model for each task

explanation.correct: Multitask fine-tuning uses datasets with examples for multiple tasks (reviews, summarization, translation, etc.). Losses calculated from these diverse examples update weights in a balanced way that maintains multi-task capability, mitigating catastrophic forgetting.

distractors.C: Freezing all weights would prevent any learning. Multitask fine-tuning does update weights — but across many tasks to maintain breadth.
distractors.D: Neural networks don't have task-specific neurons that can be selectively deleted. Weights encode knowledge distributed across the network.
distractors.E: The goal is one model handling multiple tasks, not separate models. Separate models would eliminate the efficiency benefit.

questions.stem: Which data preparation options are available in AWS for ML model training? (Select THREE)

options.text: SQL queries in SageMaker Studio via Jupyter Lab for data preparation
Apache Spark-based serverless processing through AWS Glue interactive sessions
SageMaker Feature Store for centralized feature discovery, search, and retrieval
Amazon CloudFront for data transformation at edge locations
AWS WAF for filtering training data by content category
Amazon Route 53 for routing data to appropriate processing clusters

explanation.correct: AWS provides SQL via Jupyter Lab in SageMaker Studio, Apache Spark serverless processing via Glue interactive sessions, and Feature Store for centralized feature discovery and storage in standardized format.

distractors.D: CloudFront is a CDN for content delivery, not data transformation or ML preprocessing.
distractors.E: WAF is a web application firewall. It filters web traffic, not training data content.
distractors.F: Route 53 is a DNS service for domain name resolution, not data routing for ML pipelines.

questions.stem: Which statements about RLHF fine-tuning are correct? (Select TWO)

options.text: It uses reinforcement learning to fine-tune the LLM with human feedback data
It better aligns the model with human preferences for generating responses
It eliminates the need for any pre-training before use
It replaces supervised fine-tuning entirely
It works without any human involvement

explanation.correct: RLHF uses reinforcement learning with human feedback data to fine-tune the model. This aligns model behavior with human preferences, improving performance on human-like prompts and generating human-like responses.

distractors.C: RLHF is applied to models that have already been pre-trained and often instruction-fine-tuned. It does not replace pre-training.
distractors.D: RLHF is typically applied after supervised fine-tuning, not as a replacement. It's an additional alignment step.
distractors.E: RLHF explicitly requires human involvement — humans provide the feedback/preferences that train the reward model.

questions.stem: What role do publicly available datasets and prompt template libraries play in fine-tuning? (Select TWO)

options.text: They provide ready-made training data with templates for different tasks and datasets
They reduce the effort needed to prepare instruction datasets for fine-tuning
They replace the need for any custom domain-specific data
They automatically fine-tune the model without additional configuration
They are required by AWS before any model can be deployed

explanation.correct: Many publicly available datasets and prompt template libraries have been used to train LLMs. They provide templates for different tasks and datasets, reducing the effort needed to prepare instruction datasets for fine-tuning.

distractors.C: Public datasets complement but don't replace custom domain-specific data. Domain adaptation still requires your own data.
distractors.D: Template libraries provide data, not automation. You still need to configure and run the fine-tuning process.
distractors.E: There is no AWS requirement to use public template libraries. They are optional resources to accelerate preparation.

questions.scenario: A social media company's AI content moderation model generates responses that are technically correct but often cold, unhelpful, or misaligned with how human moderators would communicate decisions to users. They want the model to be more empathetic and human-like.
questions.stem: Which fine-tuning approach would best align this model's responses with human communication preferences?

options.text: RLHF — use reinforcement learning with human feedback to align the model with human preferences for tone and communication style
Domain adaptation — train on more moderation policy documents
PEFT with LoRA — add low-rank matrices to reduce compute costs
Increase the model's temperature parameter to add randomness

explanation.correct: RLHF fine-tunes the model using human feedback to better align with human preferences. Humans can rank responses by empathy and helpfulness, training the model to generate more human-like communication.

distractors.B: Domain adaptation teaches terminology, not communication style. The model already knows the domain but needs better tone alignment.
distractors.C: LoRA is a compute efficiency technique. It doesn't specifically address alignment with human communication preferences.
distractors.D: Temperature adds randomness but doesn't teach the model preferred communication styles. It may produce more varied but equally cold responses.

questions.scenario: A CTO asks their AI team how long it would take and what it would cost to pre-train a foundation model from scratch rather than using an existing pre-trained model.
questions.stem: What should the team communicate about the complexity of pre-training from scratch?

options.text: It requires millions of GPU hours, petabytes of data, trillions of tokens, extensive trial and error, and months of time
It can be completed in a few hours with a standard laptop
It only requires downloading and installing an open-source model
It costs the same as fine-tuning since both update model weights

explanation.correct: Pre-training requires millions of GPU compute hours, terabytes to petabytes of data, trillions of tokens, trial and error, and significant time. This is why using existing pre-trained models and fine-tuning is strongly preferred.

distractors.B: Pre-training foundation models requires massive distributed computing infrastructure, not a laptop.
distractors.C: Downloading a model is using an existing pre-trained model. Pre-training from scratch is building one from the ground up.
distractors.D: Pre-training costs orders of magnitude more than fine-tuning. Pre-training can cost millions of dollars; fine-tuning can cost thousands.

questions.scenario: During fine-tuning, an ML engineer monitors the training process and observes that the loss between the model's completions and the training labels is decreasing over successive batches.
questions.stem: What does decreasing loss indicate in the fine-tuning process?

options.text: The model's completions are becoming more aligned with the training labels as weights are updated across batches
The model is losing information and forgetting all previous knowledge
The training data is being deleted from the system
The model's parameter count is decreasing

explanation.correct: Loss measures the difference between the model's completion distribution and the training label distribution. Decreasing loss means the model is improving — its outputs are becoming more aligned with expected outputs as weights are updated across batches.

distractors.B: Decreasing loss indicates improvement, not information loss. The model is learning to produce better completions.
distractors.C: Training data remains in storage. Loss is a mathematical metric computed during training, not a data deletion indicator.
distractors.D: Parameter count stays constant during fine-tuning. Loss decreasing means the existing parameters are being better optimized.

questions.scenario: An ML team completes fine-tuning and has two accuracy numbers: 92% from periodic checks during training and 89% from a final evaluation on data the model never saw during training or periodic checks.
questions.stem: Which accuracy corresponds to validation and which to test?

options.text: 92% is the validation accuracy (measured during fine-tuning); 89% is the test accuracy (final evaluation on holdout data)
92% is the test accuracy; 89% is the validation accuracy
Both are validation accuracy measured at different points
Both are test accuracy from two different test datasets

explanation.correct: Validation accuracy is measured during fine-tuning using the holdout validation dataset (92% from periodic checks). Test accuracy comes from the final performance evaluation using the holdout test dataset after fine-tuning is complete (89% on unseen data).

distractors.B: This reverses them. Validation happens during training; test happens after completion on fully held-out data.
distractors.C: They are distinct measurements from different datasets at different stages — validation during training, test after completion.
distractors.D: Test accuracy is a single final measurement. The periodic measurement during training is validation, not test.

questions.scenario: A company fine-tunes a general LLM exclusively for legal document summarization. After fine-tuning, the model produces excellent legal summaries but can no longer handle basic Q&A or translation tasks. Their CTO is concerned.
questions.stem: Should the CTO be concerned if the model is only deployed for legal summarization?

options.text: No — if the application only needs single-task legal summarization, the loss of other capabilities is an acceptable tradeoff
Yes — catastrophic forgetting always requires immediate remediation regardless of use case
Yes — the model must be retrained from scratch to fix this issue
No — the model will automatically recover its other capabilities over time

explanation.correct: If you need reliable performance on a single task (legal summarization), you might not be concerned about generalizing to other tasks. Catastrophic forgetting is acceptable when the deployment use case is narrow and well-defined.

distractors.B: Catastrophic forgetting is only a problem when multi-task capability is needed. For single-task deployment, it can be acceptable.
distractors.C: Retraining from scratch is unnecessary if the single-task performance meets requirements. The current model works well for its intended purpose.
distractors.D: Models don't automatically recover lost capabilities. The weights have been modified and won't revert on their own.

questions.scenario: A data science team preparing training data for fine-tuning wants to detect whether their dataset contains imbalanced representations or labeling biases between different demographic groups.
questions.stem: What can Amazon SageMaker Clarify detect in training data during the preparation phase? (Select TWO)

options.text: Imbalanced representations between groups such as gender, race, or age
Labeling biases that favor certain groups over others
Incorrect model architecture choices
Suboptimal hyperparameter configurations
Insufficient GPU memory for training

explanation.correct: SageMaker Clarify can analyze data and detect potential biases across multiple facets, specifically imbalanced representations and labeling biases between groups such as gender, race, or age.

distractors.C: Architecture selection is a design decision made by engineers. Clarify detects data bias, not architectural issues.
distractors.D: Hyperparameter optimization is handled by SageMaker tuning jobs. Clarify focuses on bias in data and model predictions.
distractors.E: Memory requirements are infrastructure concerns. Clarify analyzes data fairness, not compute resources.

questions.scenario: An ML architect is presenting the tradeoffs between full fine-tuning and PEFT to their team. They need to clearly explain the compute implications.
questions.stem: Why does full fine-tuning require significantly more GPU memory than PEFT? (Select TWO)

options.text: Full fine-tuning loads model parameters plus additional memory for optimizer states, gradients, and activations
Every parameter in the model is updated during full fine-tuning, requiring gradient computation for all weights
Full fine-tuning duplicates the model three times in memory
Full fine-tuning requires storing the entire training dataset in GPU memory
PEFT uses CPU-only compute while full fine-tuning uses GPU

explanation.correct: Full fine-tuning requires GPU memory for model parameters plus optimizer, gradients, forward activations, and temporal memory. Every parameter is updated, meaning gradients must be computed and stored for the entire model.

distractors.C: The model isn't duplicated three times. Additional memory comes from optimizer states and gradients, which add bytes per parameter.
distractors.D: Training data is loaded in batches, not entirely in GPU memory. The memory cost comes from model-related components.
distractors.E: Both PEFT and full fine-tuning use GPUs. PEFT simply uses less GPU memory by training fewer parameters.

questions.scenario: A team using Amazon Bedrock wants to customize Amazon Titan Text models. They want to understand their options for continuous pre-training.
questions.stem: Which Amazon Titan Text models support continued pre-training in Amazon Bedrock? (Select TWO)

options.text: Amazon Titan Text Express
Amazon Titan Text Lite
Amazon Titan Image Generator
Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings
Amazon CloudWatch Titan

explanation.correct: Continued pre-training in Amazon Bedrock helps you train Amazon Titan Text Express and Amazon Titan Text Lite FMs, customizing them with your own unlabeled data in a secure managed environment.

distractors.C: Titan Image Generator is a different model type for image generation, not mentioned as supporting continued pre-training.
distractors.D: Titan Multimodal Embeddings generates embeddings. The source specifically mentions Titan Text Express and Lite for continued pre-training.
distractors.E: There is no 'CloudWatch Titan' product. CloudWatch is a monitoring service.

questions.scenario: A data scientist needs to search across their organization's existing features, retrieve relevant ones for their new model, and store them in a centralized format accessible by all ML teams.
questions.stem: Which AWS service provides centralized feature discovery, search, retrieval, and standardized storage?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store
Amazon S3
AWS Glue Data Catalog
Amazon DynamoDB

explanation.correct: Amazon SageMaker Feature Store provides a centralized repository to search, discover, and retrieve features for model training. It stores feature data in a standardized format accessible across teams.

distractors.B: S3 provides raw data storage but not feature-specific search, discovery, or standardized ML feature formats.
distractors.C: Glue Data Catalog stores metadata about data sources. Feature Store specifically manages ML features with discovery and retrieval.
distractors.D: DynamoDB is a key-value database. It doesn't provide ML feature discovery, search, or standardized feature formats.

questions.scenario: An ML team needs to create data preparation workflows for their fine-tuning project but their data engineers are not comfortable writing code. They want a visual, low-code approach to define preprocessing and feature engineering.
questions.stem: Which AWS service provides low-code data flows for ML data preprocessing and feature engineering?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Canvas — creates data flows for ML preprocessing with little to no coding
Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth — manages labeling workflows
Amazon SageMaker Clarify — detects bias in data
Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor — monitors deployed models

explanation.correct: Amazon SageMaker Canvas provides low-code data flows that define ML data preprocessing and feature engineering workflows using little to no coding, making it accessible to data engineers without extensive programming skills.

distractors.B: Ground Truth manages data labeling workflows (human annotation), not general data preprocessing or feature engineering.
distractors.C: Clarify detects bias in data and provides explainability. It does not provide general-purpose data preprocessing workflows.
distractors.D: Model Monitor tracks model quality in production. It does not provide data preparation capabilities for training.
